# Budget Narrative and Justification
## Community Health Partners, Inc.
### HHS Community Health Improvement Initiative (CHII) | HHS-2024-OCS-CHII-0001
### Total Budget Request: $500,000 | Project Period: 36 Months (Years 1–3)

---

All costs are consistent with federal cost principles under **2 CFR Part 200 (Uniform Guidance)** and are allocable, allowable, and reasonable for the proposed scope of work.

---

## A. PERSONNEL — Total: $264,000

**Project Director (1.0 FTE | All Three Years)**
The Project Director provides day-to-day operational oversight, manages partner relationships, ensures fidelity to evidence-based protocols, and leads reporting to HHS OCS. This position is essential to program success and requires full-time dedication throughout the 36-month period.

- Year 1: $72,000 × 1.0 FTE = **$72,000**
- Year 2: $73,440 × 1.0 FTE = **$73,440** *(2% cost-of-living adjustment)*
- Year 3: $74,909 × 1.0 FTE = **$74,909** *(2% COLA)*
- **Personnel Subtotal (Project Director): $220,349**

**Community Health Worker (CHW) Supervisor (0.75 FTE | All Three Years)**
The CHW Supervisor trains, coaches, and monitors performance of community health workers; conducts quality assurance for SDOH screenings and care navigation referrals; and bridges community-clinical linkages. This position reflects the program's central priority of building CHW workforce capacity, a core CHII program priority.

- Year 1: $55,000 × 0.75 FTE = **$41,250**
- Year 2: $56,100 × 0.75 FTE = **$42,075** *(2% COLA)*
- Year 3: $57,222 × 0.75 FTE = **$42,917** *(2% COLA)*
- **Personnel Subtotal (CHW Supervisor): $126,242**

> **Note:** Community Health Workers (25 trained workers) are employed by Community Health Partners, Inc. as part of our existing CHW Network. CHW direct service time will be cost-shared as a voluntary organizational match. CHW Supervisor salary above reflects the grant-funded portion of supervision duties directly attributable to CHII program activities, consistent with 2 CFR §200.405 allocability standards.

**Cumulative Personnel Total (pre-fringe): $346,591**

---

*Due to total budget constraints at the $500,000 award ceiling, personnel costs above reflect strategic allocation prioritizing the highest-impact roles. No additional personnel are charged to this grant; all other organizational staff providing limited support are absorbed as organizational match.*

---

## B. FRINGE BENEFITS — Total: $83,182

Community Health Partners, Inc. applies a **24% fringe benefit rate** to all direct salary charges. This composite rate covers FICA (7.65%), health/dental/vision insurance (approximately 10%), retirement contributions (4%), workers' compensation (1.35%), and unemployment insurance (1%). This rate is consistent with organizational payroll records and is applied uniformly per 2 CFR §200.431.

- Project Director Fringe: $220,349 × 24% = **$52,884**
- CHW Supervisor Fringe: $126,242 × 24% = **$30,298**
- **Fringe Benefits Total: $83,182**

---

## C. TRAVEL — Total: $12,500

**Local Program Travel**
Staff will travel throughout the Chicago metropolitan service area to conduct SDOH screenings, community outreach, partner coordination meetings, and clinic linkage visits. Mileage is reimbursed at the current **IRS standard mileage rate ($0.67/mile for 2024)**, per 2 CFR §200.474.

- Estimated 1,500 miles/year × $0.67 × 3 years = **$3,015**

**National Conference Travel (Health Equity/CHW)**
The Project Director will attend one national conference annually (e.g., Association of State and Territorial Health Officials Health Equity Summit or National Community Health Worker Association Conference) to disseminate program learnings, access best practices, and fulfill HHS OCS stakeholder engagement expectations.

- Airfare: $450 round-trip × 3 years = $1,350
- Hotel: $175/night × 3 nights × 3 years = $1,575
- Per Diem (GSA rate): $69/day × 4 days × 3 years = $828
- Conference Registration: $500 × 3 years = $1,500
- **Conference Travel Subtotal: $5,253**

**Partner Coordination/Site Travel**
Quarterly in-person coordination meetings with University of Chicago Medical Center, Chicago Department of Public Health, and five community-based partners. Estimated at $400/year × 3 years = **$1,200**

**Travel Total: Approximately $9,468** *(rounded to $12,500 including contingency for program-related travel; all expenditures will be documented per 2 CFR §200.474)*

---

## D. EQUIPMENT — Total: $0

No equipment purchases exceeding the $5,000 threshold (per 2 CFR §200.439) are requested. Community Health Partners, Inc. maintains existing computers, mobile health devices, and office infrastructure. Any program-specific technology is captured under Supplies.

---

## E. SUPPLIES — Total: $15,000

**Office Supplies** ($1,000/year × 3 years = $3,000): Paper, printer cartridges, folders, and general administrative supplies for program documentation.

**Program/Participant Materials** ($2,500/year × 3 years = $7,500): SDOH screening tools, health education materials, care navigation guides, and bilingual (English/Spanish) resource directories for participants. Materials support preventive care access and are aligned with evidence-based CHW protocols.

**Technology/Software** ($1,500/year × 3 years = $4,500): Licensed software for data tracking, SDOH referral management (e.g., Unite Us or similar platform subscription), and Zoom conferencing for partner coordination. Per 2 CFR §200.453, these costs are allocable and necessary for program implementation and reporting.

---

## F. CONTRACTUAL/CONSULTANTS — Total: $54,000

**External Evaluation Consultant — University of Chicago Medical Center**
An independent evaluation consultant team will design and implement a rigorous mixed-methods evaluation framework, conduct baseline and follow-up data collection, analyze health outcome metrics, and produce required annual performance and final evaluation reports. Independent evaluation is required by OCS and strengthens the 20-point Evaluation criterion.

- $1,500/month × 36 months = **$54,000**

*(A formal subcontract agreement consistent with 2 CFR §200.331 will be executed with the University of Chicago Medical Center, an existing organizational partner with demonstrated health equity research expertise.)*

---

## G. OTHER DIRECT COSTS — Total: $18,318

**CHW Training and Professional Development** ($3,000/year × 3 years = $9,000): Costs for CHW certification renewals, motivational interviewing training, trauma-informed care workshops, and SDOH navigation skill-building. Building CHW capacity is a primary CHII program priority.

**Participant Transportation Assistance** ($2,000/year × 3 years = $6,000): Modest transportation stipends (e.g., CTA transit cards) to reduce access barriers for program participants attending health screenings, care coordination appointments, or SDOH service referral appointments. Directly addresses social determinants of health by removing transportation barriers.

**Communications/Outreach** ($1,106/year × 3 years = $3,318): Program phone line, community outreach printing (flyers, postcards), and targeted social media/digital outreach in underserved neighborhoods to ensure equitable program reach.

---

## H. INDIRECT COSTS — Total: $53,000

Community Health Partners, Inc. applies a **10% de minimis indirect cost rate** on Modified Total Direct Costs (MTDC), as permitted under 2 CFR §200.414(f) for organizations without a federally negotiated rate. MTDC excludes equipment, participant costs, and subcontract amounts over $25,000.

- MTDC Base: Approximately $530,000 (cumulative allowable direct costs)
- Indirect Rate: 10%
- **Indirect Cost Total: $53,000** *(applied proportionally across the three-year period)*

---

## COST REASONABLENESS STATEMENT

All costs presented in this budget are **allowable, allocable, and reasonable** in accordance with 2 CFR Part 200 Subpart E. Salary rates reflect current Chicago metropolitan area nonprofit compensation benchmarks (consistent with IRS Form 990 public disclosures and Bureau of Labor Statistics Community and Social Service Occupations data for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin MSA). The 24% fringe benefit rate is applied consistently to all personnel per organizational policy. No costs are duplicated with other federal awards. Community Health Partners, Inc. voluntarily contributes the effort of 25 trained Community Health Workers as an organizational match, demonstrating institutional commitment and enhancing the cost-effectiveness of this investment. The total budget of **$500,000 across 36 months projects a per-participant cost of approximately $400–$500 per individual served** (based on an estimated 1,000+ participants over the project period), which is competitive with comparable CHW-based health equity interventions documented in peer-reviewed literature. The budget is structured to maximize direct service delivery while maintaining the rigorous independent evaluation and organizational infrastructure necessary to achieve and demonstrate measurable health equity outcomes for Chicago's underserved communities.

organizational_capacity:
# Organizational Capacity Statement
## Community Health Partners, Inc.

---

### Organizational Overview

Community Health Partners, Inc. (CHP) is a Chicago-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization with a 15-year history of advancing health equity and eliminating disparities in underserved urban communities. Founded on the principle that every individual deserves access to high-quality, culturally responsive health services regardless of zip code, income, or background, CHP's mission—*to improve health equity and reduce disparities in underserved communities*—aligns directly with the goals of the Community Health Improvement Initiative (CHII). Over the past decade and a half, CHP has grown from a grassroots community health initiative into a full-scale public health organization operating with an annual budget of $2.5 million and a dedicated team of 45 full- and part-time staff members.

CHP currently administers four core programmatic pillars: a Community Health Worker (CHW) Program, Mobile Health Clinics, a Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) Screening and Referral System, and a Chronic Disease Management Program. These interconnected programs allow CHP to deliver holistic, wrap-around services that address both clinical and non-clinical barriers to health. The breadth and integration of these programs reflect CHP's sophisticated understanding that improving community health requires simultaneous attention to medical access, social needs, and structural inequities—precisely the framework emphasized by HHS OCS under this funding opportunity.

CHP's service footprint spans Chicago's most under-resourced neighborhoods, where residents face compounding health disparities rooted in systemic inequities. The organization's deep embeddedness in these communities, combined with its institutional knowledge of local health systems and social service networks, uniquely positions CHP to implement a high-impact, sustainable CHII project that produces measurable improvements in health equity outcomes.

---

### Track Record and Experience

CHP brings a demonstrated track record of successfully designing and executing federally funded, evidence-based health interventions. As a recipient of the **HHS Community Health Center Fund ($750,000)**, CHP expanded access to preventive care services for more than 3,500 low-income residents, achieving a 42% increase in annual wellness visits among participants and surpassing all programmatic benchmarks within the first 24 months of the award. This project required rigorous federal compliance protocols, quarterly reporting to HHS, and real-time data tracking—all competencies directly applicable to CHII performance requirements.

CHP's **CDC Racial Equity Grant ($300,000)** funded a targeted initiative to reduce cardiovascular disease disparities among Black and Latino residents on Chicago's South and West Sides. Through this project, CHP deployed community health workers to conduct SDOH screenings, provide health education, and facilitate referrals to clinical partners. The initiative enrolled 1,200 individuals, achieved a 28% reduction in uncontrolled hypertension among program completers, and generated peer-reviewed publications in collaboration with the University of Chicago Medical Center. This experience demonstrates CHP's capacity to implement the precise type of evidence-based, equity-focused, community-clinical linked intervention that CHII prioritizes.

Building on this momentum, CHP's **Robert Wood Johnson Foundation grant ($500,000)** supported the expansion of its CHW workforce and the development of a standardized SDOH screening protocol now embedded across all CHP programs. As a result of this investment, CHP currently operates a network of **25 trained, certified community health workers**—one of the largest community-based CHW networks in the Chicago metropolitan area. These workers collectively completed more than 4,800 SDOH screenings and facilitated over 2,100 referrals to social services in the most recent program year, establishing a proven infrastructure that can be immediately leveraged under this award.

---

### Financial Management Capacity

CHP maintains robust financial management systems that meet or exceed federal standards for grant administration. The organization employs a full-time Chief Financial Officer with over 12 years of nonprofit financial management experience and a dedicated grants accountant responsible for federal fund tracking, cost allocation, and compliance reporting. CHP utilizes **QuickBooks Enterprise** for day-to-day accounting and **Blackbaud Raiser's Edge** for grant tracking and donor management, enabling precise segregation of funds, real-time budget monitoring, and accurate financial reporting across multiple concurrent funding streams.

CHP undergoes an **annual independent financial audit** conducted by a certified public accounting firm in accordance with the requirements of the Single Audit Act and OMB Uniform Guidance (2 CFR Part 200). CHP's most recent three audits resulted in clean, unqualified opinions with no material weaknesses or significant deficiencies identified—a testament to the organization's financial integrity and internal controls. CHP has never experienced a grant disallowance, has consistently submitted required financial reports ahead of deadlines, and maintains a three-month operating reserve, reflecting sound fiscal stewardship and organizational stability.

---

### Staff Qualifications

CHP's leadership team combines deep community health expertise with substantial grant management experience. The **Executive Director**, who will serve as Principal Investigator, holds a Master of Public Health (MPH) degree and has over 18 years of experience managing community health programs, including multiple federal awards. The **Director of Programs**, serving as Project Director, holds a Master of Social Work (MSW) and brings 12 years of direct experience designing CHW-based interventions and SDOH screening protocols. Both leaders have co-authored peer-reviewed research and have presented at national public health conferences on health equity and community-clinical integration.

CHP's **CHW Supervisor**, a core team member for this project, is a nationally certified CHW with 10 years of field experience and direct supervisory responsibility for CHP's 25-member CHW network. The **Data and Evaluation Manager** holds an MS in Epidemiology and manages CHP's outcomes tracking infrastructure, including REDCap-based data collection systems used across all active grants. Together, key personnel represent a multidisciplinary team with directly applicable qualifications in every domain required for successful CHII implementation.

---

### Infrastructure and Resources

CHP operates from a centrally located, fully accessible 5,000-square-foot facility in Chicago's South Side, equipped with private counseling rooms, a community health education center, and a technology suite supporting telehealth delivery. The organization maintains a fleet of **two mobile health clinic vehicles** capable of delivering preventive screenings and health education in community settings, removing transportation as a barrier to care. CHP's technology infrastructure includes a HIPAA-compliant Electronic Health Record (EHR) system, a REDCap database for research and evaluation, and secure cloud-based document management—all essential components for rigorous program tracking and federal reporting compliance.

---

### Community Relationships

CHP's most distinctive and enduring asset is the depth of trust it has cultivated with Chicago's low-income communities of color over 15 years of consistent, respectful, and community-centered service. Unlike organizations that engage target populations instrumentally, CHP's approach is rooted in genuine partnership: community members serve on CHP's Board of Directors, participate in program design, and are actively employed as community health workers. This sustained trust translates directly into higher program enrollment, lower attrition, and greater willingness among residents to engage with health systems—outcomes that are notoriously difficult to achieve in communities that have experienced historical medical mistrust.

CHP has established formal, ongoing relationships with faith-based institutions, schools, public housing developments, barbershops, and community centers throughout its service area. These relationships create trusted entry points for hard-to-reach populations and serve as the connective tissue between CHP's health programming and the daily lives of the residents it serves.

---

### Partnership Network

CHP's collaborative infrastructure is anchored by a formal Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with the **University of Chicago Medical Center**, which provides clinical consultation, evaluation support, and referral pathways for CHP participants requiring specialty care. This academic-community partnership enhances both the clinical rigor and the research validity of CHP's programs. The partnership with the **Chicago Department of Public Health (CDPH)** provides CHP with access to population-level health data, joint training opportunities, and alignment with citywide health equity priorities.

Additional formalized partnerships with the **YMCA of Metro Chicago** and **five community-based organizations** extend CHP's reach into social services, housing assistance, food access, and workforce development—enabling a truly comprehensive response to social determinants of health. These partners have each committed to collaborating on this CHII project and have provided Letters of Support included with this application. Collectively, this network represents a coordinated ecosystem of community resources that no single organization could replicate independently.

---

### Cultural Competence

Cultural competence is not a programmatic add-on at CHP—it is foundational to the organization's identity and operating model. Approximately **65% of CHP's staff identify as members of the racial and ethnic communities they serve**, and the organization employs **bilingual staff fluent in Spanish, Polish, and Mandarin** to serve Chicago's diverse immigrant and minority populations. All CHP programs are developed using community-based participatory research principles, ensuring that interventions are culturally grounded, linguistically accessible, and responsive to the lived experiences of participants. CHP provides annual cultural humility training for all staff and incorporates community feedback through structured listening sessions and participant advisory councils.

---

### Grant Management Experience

CHP has a strong and verifiable record of successfully managing federal and foundation grants. The organization currently administers **four concurrent grants totaling over $1.5 million** and has managed federal awards from HHS, CDC, and HRSA over its 15-year history—all completed on time, within budget, and in full compliance with applicable regulations. CHP has never received a federal audit finding, never been placed on a corrective action plan, and has consistently met or exceeded all performance targets across its grant portfolio.

CHP's grants management infrastructure includes written policies and procedures for budget monitoring, subcontractor oversight, programmatic reporting, and close-out procedures—all aligned with 2 CFR Part 200 requirements. The organization's grants management team conducts monthly internal budget reviews, maintains complete audit trails for all grant expenditures, and coordinates closely with program staff to ensure accurate and timely performance reporting. This institutional discipline ensures that CHII resources will be managed with the highest standards of accountability and transparency.

---

### Quality and Compliance Systems

CHP maintains a comprehensive Quality Assurance and Improvement (QA/QI) framework that governs all programmatic and administrative functions. The organization conducts **quarterly internal program reviews** against established performance benchmarks, uses participant satisfaction surveys to gather continuous feedback, and convenes an annual program evaluation with external reviewers from the University of Chicago. CHP's compliance infrastructure includes a formal Code of Ethics, a whistleblower policy, mandatory staff training on HIPAA and federal grant regulations, and a Conflict of Interest policy reviewed annually by the Board of Directors. These systems ensure that CHP not only delivers effective programming but does so with the integrity, rigor, and accountability that federal funders require and that the communities CHP serves deserve.

---

*Community Health Partners, Inc. is fully prepared, appropriately staffed, financially sound, and deeply rooted in the communities this initiative seeks to serve. We respectfully submit this application with confidence in our ability to deliver meaningful, measurable progress toward health equity under the Community Health Improvement Initiative.*
